items.title: Rental car charges and a StubHub scalping scheme exposed: CBC's Marketplace Cheat Sheet
items.summary_auto: The CBC's Marketplace rounds up the consumer and health news you need from the week.

items.title: How a family is coping a year after toddler nearly killed in Lapu-Lapu Day tragedy
items.summary_auto: Val De La Cruz and his son Jeb were lining up for a lemonade at the Lapu-Lapu Day festival last April when the two were struck by an SUV ramming through the crowd. One year on, the 2 ½-year-old is back to giggling and singing, but his parents are still dealing with injuries and fear.

items.title: White House correspondents' dinner shooting suspect a part-time teacher, game developer
items.summary_auto: The chief of the ​District of Columbia police department said investigators believe the suspect was a guest at the Washington Hilton hotel, where the annual dinner was ​taking place, but that no motive had been determined.

items.title: This Acadia student didn't like cooking. Now his Nepalese dumplings are in demand
items.summary_auto: Acadia University student Sujit Acharya owed thousands in tuition and had a few hundred dollars to his name when he decided to make some Nepalese dumplings for a festival in the hopes to earn some money. The business now sells its products at farmers' markets and in several stores. And that's just the start of his plans.

items.title: Toronto General Hospital says partnerships helping with emergency department volume
items.summary_auto:
At UHN's Toronto General Hospital, staff say being right downtown comes with its own unique set of challenges,
and it's working alongside other organizations to help alleviate some of the demand in its emergency department.

items.title: 1 reason why healthy people have heart attacks, and what experts say could help
items.summary_auto: Research indicates that about one in five Canadians have high levels of Lipoprotein(a), putting them at greater risk of a heart attack or stroke. And yet, many people have no idea because there is no routine screening for Lp(a), which also has no symptoms. That's something experts have been wanting to change.

items.title: From National Pizza Day to National Puppy Day: Why so many national days have filled the calendar
items.summary_auto: National Day Calendar recognizes over 3,000 special days, weeks and months that are celebrated worldwide. The days honour everything from jelly beans to beer cans — but if every day is special, is any day truly special?

items.title: Facing rural realities and mistrust in hospitals, these women turned to home births
items.summary_auto: Though no one is tracking medically unassisted births in Canada, infant mortality data from the Ontario coroner, the challenges of accessing maternal medicine in rural B.C., and a growing online freebirthing community suggest more women are choosing this route.

items.title: After autistic child's wandering from school, experts say Ontario's special education needs more support
items.summary_auto: Elopement, when an autistic person runs or wanders away from caregivers or a safe location, is a regular school safety consideration. Yet advocates and experts say the elopement of a nine-year-old from school this week puts the spotlight on inadequate in-school support of Ontario students with disabilities.

items.title: When my sons with autism are sent home from school, they've learned the problem is them — not the system
items.summary_auto: Wendy Lintner's twin sons with autism are in Grade 5. At one time, both were not attending school full-time. Not because she doesn’t believe in education or because they don't want to be there, but because the school system simply hasn’t been able to support their needs.

items.title: Albertans call for answers after being stranded on northern highway during snow storm
items.summary_auto: Highway 63 reopened Saturday after heavy snow and strong winds left motorists stranded on Friday, but some people that were stuck on the road say it took the province, local government and RCMP too long to act.

items.title: Manitoba to ban social media, AI chatbots for youth, premier says
items.summary_auto: Manitoba will ban youth from using social media and AI chatbots, Premier Wab Kinew announced on Saturday. The proposed law protecting youth from the harmful effects of social media will be the first of its kind in Canada.

items.title: Shooting suspect at correspondents' dinner was likely targeting Trump officials, says acting attorney general
Trump unharmed after 'shooting incident' at White House correspondents' dinner
items.summary_auto: A man armed with guns and knives stormed the lobby outside a high-profile journalists' dinner attended by U.S. President Donald Trump and multiple senior U.S. leaders on Saturday night, rushing toward the ballroom before Secret Service agents swarmed him and took him into custody.

items.title: Pro rock climber dies after falling 20 metres from Stawamus Chief in Squamish, B.C.
items.summary_auto: Will Stanhope was widely known as a free solo climber and was a member of the Association of Canadian Mountain Guides. He was scaling Rutabaga at the time of his fall.

items.title: Professional climber dies after 20 metre fall from Stawamus Chief in Squamish
items.summary_auto: Will Stanhope was widely known as a free solo climber and was a member of the Association of Canadian Mountain Guides. He was scaling Rutabaga at the time of his fall.

items.title: NHL playoffs: Fans are following the games — and the WAGs
items.summary_auto: Interest in wives and girlfriends of professional athletes — known as "WAGs" — is a global phenomenon, across sports from hockey to soccer to F1 racing. Experts say social media has created opportunities for WAGs to build their own brands and expand their influence — and thus, create names for themselves in their own right.
